He is unsmiling, looking past the camera.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Warren_Buffett_at_the_2015_SelectUSA_Investment_Summit_%28cropped%29.jpg/500px-Warren_Buffett_at_the_2015_SelectUSA_Investment_Summit_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Warren_Buffett_at_the_2015_SelectUSA_Investment_Summit_(cropped).jpg)

Buffett") (cousin) | | Website | [berkshirehathaway.com](http://berkshirehathaway.com/) | | Signature | | [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Warren_Buffett_Signature.svg/330px-Warren_Buffett_Signature.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Warren_Buffett_Signature.svg) | **Warren Edward Buffett** ([/ˈbʌfɪt/](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [_BUFF-it_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); born August 30, 1930)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-biography.com-2) is an American investor and philanthropist who is the chairman and former CEO of the conglomerate [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway"). As a result of his success, Buffett is one of the best-known investors in the world. According to _[Forbes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")_, as of January 2026, Buffett's estimated net worth stood at US$148.9 billion, making him the ninth-richest person in the world.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Forbes-3) Buffett was born in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"). The son of [U.S. congressman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and businessman [Howard Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_Buffett "Howard Buffett"), he developed an interest in business and investing during his youth. He entered the [Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wharton_School_of_the_University_of_Pennsylvania "Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania") in 1947 before graduating from the [University of Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln") in [Lincoln](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lincoln,_Nebraska "Lincoln, Nebraska") at 20. He went on to graduate from [Columbia Business School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbia_Business_School "Columbia Business School"), where he molded his investment [philosophy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") around the concept of [value investing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_investing "Value investing") pioneered by [Benjamin Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benjamin_Graham "Benjamin Graham"). He attended [New York Institute of Finance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Finance "New York Institute of Finance") to focus on his economics background and soon pursued a business career. He then began several business ventures and investment partnerships, including one with Graham. He created Buffett Partnership Ltd. in 1956 and his investment firm eventually acquired textile manufacturer Berkshire Hathaway, applying its name to a diversified [holding company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holding_company "Holding company"). Buffett emerged as the company's chairman and majority shareholder in 1970. In 1978, fellow investor and long-time business associate [Charlie Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Munger "Charlie Munger") joined Buffett as vice-chairman.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-4)[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-5) From 1970 to 2026, Buffett presided as the chairman and largest shareholder of Berkshire Hathaway, one of America's [foremost holding companies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lists_of_corporate_assets#Holding_companies "Lists of corporate assets") and [world's leading corporate conglomerates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_public_corporations_by_market_capitalization#2022 "List of public corporations by market capitalization"). He has been referred to as the "Oracle" or "Sage" of [Omaha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha "Omaha") by global media as a result of having accumulated a massive fortune derived from his business and investment success.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-6)[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-7) Buffett adheres to the principles of value investing and frugality despite his wealth.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-8) Buffett has pledged to give away 99 percent[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-9) of his fortune to philanthropic causes, primarily via the [Gates Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gates_Foundation "Gates Foundation"). He founded [The Giving Pledge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Giving_Pledge "The Giving Pledge") in 2010, with [Bill Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") and [Melinda French Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melinda_French_Gates "Melinda French Gates"), whereby billionaires pledge to give away at least half of their fortunes.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-bbc-10) At Berkshire Hathaway's investor conference on May 3, 2025, Buffett requested that the board appoint [Greg Abel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Abel "Greg Abel") to succeed him as the company's chief executive officer by the year's end while he remained chairman.[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-11) ## Early life and education Buffett was born on August 30, 1930, in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), as the second of three children and the only son of [Congressman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") [Howard Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_Buffett "Howard Buffett") and his wife, Leila (née Stahl) Buffett.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-12) He began his education at Rose Hill Elementary School. In 1942, his father was elected to the first of four terms in the [United States Congress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), and after moving with his family to Washington, D.C., Warren finished elementary school, attended Alice Deal Junior High School and graduated from what was then [Woodrow Wilson High School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackson-Reed_High_School "Jackson-Reed High School") in 1947, where his senior yearbook picture reads: "likes math; a future stockbroker".[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-13) After finishing high school and finding success with his side entrepreneurial and investment ventures, Buffett wanted to skip college to go directly into business but was overruled by his father.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-14)[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-15) Buffett showcased an interest in business and investing at a young age. He was inspired by a book he borrowed from the Omaha public library at age seven, _[One Thousand Ways to Make $1000](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/One_Thousand_Ways_to_Make_$1000 "One Thousand Ways to Make $1000")_.[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-16) Much of Buffett's early childhood years were enlivened with entrepreneurial ventures. In one of his first business ventures, Buffett sold chewing gum, [Coca-Cola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola"), and weekly magazines [door to door](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Door_to_door "Door to door"). He worked in his grandfather's grocery store. While still in high school, he made money delivering newspapers, selling golf balls and stamps, and detailing cars, among other means. On his first income [tax return](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tax_return_(United_States) "Tax return (United States)") in 1944, Buffett took a $35 deduction (equivalent to $640in 2025) for the use of his bicycle and watch on his paper route.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-17) In 1945, as a high school sophomore, Buffett and a friend spent $25 (equivalent to $447in 2025) to purchase a used [pinball](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinball "Pinball") machine, which they placed in the local [barber shop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barber "Barber"). Within months, they owned several machines in three different barber shops across Omaha. In 1947 they sold the business to a war veteran for $1,200 (equivalent to $17,303in 2025).[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Warren_Buffett_Timeline-18) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Benjamin_Graham_%281894-1976%29_in_1950.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Benjamin_Graham_(1894-1976)_in_1950.jpg) Investor [Benjamin Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benjamin_Graham "Benjamin Graham") (pictured) was a major influential figure on the young Warren Buffett. Buffett's interest in the stock market and investing dated back to his schoolboy days spent in the customers' lounge of a regional [stock brokerage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brokerage_firm "Brokerage firm") near his father's own brokerage office. His father took interest in cultivating and educating the young Warren's curiosity surrounding the subject of business and investing, even at one point taking him to visit the [New York Stock Exchange](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") when he was 10.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-19) At 11, he bought three shares of [Cities Service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cities_Service "Cities Service") Preferred for himself, and three for his sister [Doris Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doris_Buffett "Doris Buffett") (who also became a philanthropist).[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-20)[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-21)[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-22) At 15, Warren made more than $175 monthly (equivalent to $3,130in 2025) delivering _Washington Post_ newspapers. In high school, he invested in a business owned by his father and bought a 40-acre farm worked by a tenant farmer.[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:1-23) He bought the land when he was 14 years old with $1,200 (equivalent to $21,947in 2025) of his savings.[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:1-23) By the time he graduated from college, Buffett had amassed $9,800 in savings (equivalent to $132,608in 2025).[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Warren_Buffett_Timeline-18) In 1947, Buffett matriculated at the [Wharton School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wharton_School "Wharton School") of the [University of Pennsylvania](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"). He would have preferred to focus on his business ventures, but enrolled due to pressure from his father.[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Warren_Buffett_Timeline-18) Warren studied there for two years and joined the [Alpha Sigma Phi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_Sigma_Phi "Alpha Sigma Phi") fraternity.[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-24) He then transferred to the [University of Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln") where he graduated with a [Bachelor of Science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") in [Business Administration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business_administration "Business administration") with major in [Investment Management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Investment_management "Investment management") in 1951.[\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-25)[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-26) After being rejected by [Harvard Business School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School") in the spring of 1950, Buffett enrolled at [Columbia Business School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbia_Business_School "Columbia Business School") of [Columbia University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") upon learning that [Benjamin Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benjamin_Graham "Benjamin Graham") taught there. He earned a [Master of Science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Master_of_Science "Master of Science") in [Economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economics "Economics") from Columbia in 1951.[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-27)[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-28) After graduating, Buffett attended the [New York Institute of Finance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Finance "New York Institute of Finance").[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-29)[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-30) > The basic ideas of investing are to look at stocks as business, use the market's fluctuations to your advantage, and seek a [margin of safety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Margin_of_safety_(financial) "Margin of safety (financial)"). That's what Ben Graham taught us. A hundred years from now they will still be the cornerstones of investing.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-31)[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-FinancialPost_20120424-32)[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-NASDAQ_20130115-33) —Warren Buffett ## Business career Further informationon Warren Buffett's time at Berkshire Hathaway: [List of assets owned by Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_assets_owned_by_Berkshire_Hathaway "List of assets owned by Berkshire Hathaway") ### Early business career Buffett worked from 1951 to 1954 at his father's firm, Buffett-Falk & Co., as an investment salesman; from 1954 to 1956 at Graham-Newman Corp. as a securities analyst; from 1956 to 1969 at several investment partnerships as the general partner; and from 1970 as [chairman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chair_(official) "Chair (official)") and CEO of [Berkshire Hathaway Inc](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway"). In 1951, Buffett discovered that [Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benjamin_Graham "Benjamin Graham") was on the board of [GEICO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEICO "GEICO") insurance. Taking a train to Washington, D.C., on a Saturday, he knocked on the door of GEICO's headquarters until a janitor admitted him. There he met Lorimer Davidson, GEICO's vice president, and the two discussed the insurance business for hours, and Buffett made his first purchase of GEICO stock.[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-34) Davidson would eventually become Buffett's lifelong friend and a lasting influence,[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-35) and would later recall that he found Buffett to be an "extraordinary man" after only fifteen minutes. Buffett wanted to work on [Wall Street](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") but both his father and Ben Graham urged him not to. He offered to work for Graham for free, but Graham refused.[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-36) Buffett returned to Omaha and worked as a stockbroker while taking a [Dale Carnegie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dale_Carnegie "Dale Carnegie") public speaking course.[\[37\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-37) Using what he learned, he felt confident enough to teach an "Investment Principles" night class at the [University of Nebraska-Omaha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Nebraska-Omaha "University of Nebraska-Omaha"). The average age of his students was more than twice his own. During this time he also purchased a Sinclair gas station as a side investment but it was unsuccessful.[\[38\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-38) In 1954, Buffett accepted a job at [Benjamin Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benjamin_Graham "Benjamin Graham")'s partnership. His starting salary was $12,000 a year (equivalent to $143,866in 2025).[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-inflation-US-39) There he worked closely with [Walter Schloss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_Schloss "Walter Schloss"). Graham was adamant that stock picks should provide a wide [margin of safety](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Margin_of_safety_(financial) "Margin of safety (financial)") after weighing the trade-off between their price and their intrinsic value. In 1956, Benjamin Graham retired and closed his partnership. At this time Buffett, who had amassed personal savings over $174,000 (equivalent to $2,060,526in 2025),[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-inflation-US-39) decided to return to Omaha, where he would quickly start a series of investment partnerships. In 1957, Buffett operated three investment partnerships. By 1959, the total had grown to six partnerships. That summer, Buffett was introduced to his future partner [Charlie Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Munger "Charlie Munger") during a business luncheon at The [Omaha Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha_Club "Omaha Club").[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-40) In 1961, Buffett revealed that 35% of the partnership's assets were invested in the [Sanborn Map Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sanborn_Map_Company "Sanborn Map Company"). He explained that Sanborn stock sold for only $45 per share in 1958 (equivalent to $502in 2025), but the company's investment portfolio was worth $65 per share (equivalent to $725in 2025). This meant that Sanborn's map business was being valued at "minus $20". Buffett eventually purchased 23% of the company's outstanding shares as an [activist investor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Activist_investor "Activist investor"), obtaining a seat for himself on the board of directors, and allied with other dissatisfied shareholders to control 44% of the shares. To avoid a [proxy fight](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proxy_fight "Proxy fight"), the board offered to [repurchase shares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Share_repurchase "Share repurchase") at fair value, paying with a portion of its investment portfolio. 77% of the outstanding shares were turned in.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-buffett1960-41)[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-42) Buffett had reaped a 50 percent return on investment in just two years.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-43) ### Assuming Berkshire In 1962, Buffett became a millionaire with the success of his partnerships, which by then had grown to 11 entities and held nearly $7.2 million (equivalent to $76,633,663in 2025), of which more than $1,025,000 (equivalent to $10,910,000in 2025) belonged to Buffett. At the start of the year, he merged the various partnerships into the single entity Buffett Partnership, Ltd., which would be his primary investment vehicle for the remainder of the decade.[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-44) Buffett invested in and eventually took control of a textile manufacturing company, [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway"). He began buying shares in Berkshire from [Seabury Stanton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seabury_Stanton "Seabury Stanton"), the owner, who quit due to policy disagreements with the new majority shareholder.[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-45) Buffett's partnerships began purchasing shares at $7.60 per share (equivalent to $81in 2025). In 1965, when Buffett's partnerships began purchasing Berkshire aggressively, they paid $14.86 per share (equivalent to $152in 2025) while the company had [working capital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_capital "Working capital") of $19 per share (equivalent to $194in 2025). This did not include the value of fixed assets (factory and equipment). Buffett took control of Berkshire Hathaway at a board meeting and named a new president, Ken Chace, to run the company. In 1966, Buffett closed the partnership to new money. He later claimed that the textile business had been his worst trade.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-46) He then moved the business into the insurance sector, and, in 1985, the last of the mills that had been the core business of Berkshire Hathaway was sold. In a second letter, Buffett announced his first investment in a private business — Hochschild, Kohn and Co, a privately owned Baltimore department store. In 1967, Berkshire paid out its first and only dividend of 10 cents.[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Kenny2009-47) In 1969, Buffett liquidated the partnership and transferred their assets to his partners including shares of Berkshire Hathaway. He lived solely on his salary of $50,000 per year (equivalent to $438,975in 2025) and his outside investment income. In 1973, Berkshire began to acquire stock in the [Washington Post Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graham_Holdings_Company "Graham Holdings Company"). Buffett became close friends with [Katharine Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Katharine_Graham "Katharine Graham"), who controlled the company and its flagship newspaper and joined its board. In 1974, the SEC opened a formal investigation into Buffett and Berkshire's acquisition of [Wesco Financial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wesco_Financial "Wesco Financial"), due to possible conflict of interest. No charges were brought. In 1977, Berkshire [indirectly purchased](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Berkshire_Hathaway_publications "List of Berkshire Hathaway publications") the _[Buffalo Evening News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffalo_Evening_News "Buffalo Evening News")_ for $32.5 million (equivalent to $172,672,982in 2025). Antitrust charges started, instigated by its rival, the _[Buffalo Courier-Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffalo_Courier-Express "Buffalo Courier-Express")_. Both papers lost money until the _Courier-Express_ folded in 1982. In 1979, Berkshire began to acquire stock in [ABC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"). [Capital Cities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capital_Cities_Communications "Capital Cities Communications") announced a $3.5 billion (equivalent to $15,526,134,801in 2025) purchase of ABC on March 18, 1985, surprising the media industry, as ABC was four times bigger than Capital Cities at the time. Buffett helped finance the deal in return for a 25% stake in the combined company.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-48) The newly merged company, known as Capital Cities/ABC (or CapCities/ABC), was forced to sell some stations due to [Federal Communications Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") ownership rules. The two companies also owned several radio stations in the same markets.[\[49\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-49) In 1987, Berkshire Hathaway purchased a 12% stake in Salomon Inc., making it the largest shareholder and Buffett a director. In 1990, a scandal involving [John Gutfreund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Gutfreund "John Gutfreund") (former CEO of [Salomon Brothers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salomon_Brothers "Salomon Brothers")) surfaced. A [rogue trader](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rogue_trader "Rogue trader"), Paul Mozer, was submitting bids in excess of what was allowed by Treasury rules. When this was brought to Gutfreund's attention, he did not immediately suspend the rogue trader. Gutfreund left the company in August 1991.[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-50) Buffett became chairman of Salomon until the crisis passed.[\[51\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-51) In 1988, Buffett began buying [The Coca-Cola Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Coca-Cola_Company "The Coca-Cola Company") stock, eventually purchasing up to 7% of the company for $1.02billion (equivalent to $2,776,730,445in 2025).[\[52\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-52) It would turn out to be one of Berkshire's most lucrative investments and one which it still holds.[\[53\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-53) ### As a billionaire In 1998 Buffett acquired [General Re](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Re "General Re") (Gen Re) as a subsidiary in a deal that presented difficulties — according to the _Rational Walk_ investment website, "underwriting standards proved to be inadequate", while a "problematic derivatives book" was resolved after numerous years and a significant loss.[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Walk-54) Gen Re later provided [reinsurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reinsurance "Reinsurance") after Buffett became involved with [Maurice R. Greenberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maurice_R._Greenberg "Maurice R. Greenberg") at [AIG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AIG "AIG") in 2002.[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-55) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/P071811PS-0254_%285951720542%29.jpg/960px-P071811PS-0254_%285951720542%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:P071811PS-0254_(5951720542).jpg) With President [Barack Obama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") at the White House in July 2011 During a 2005 investigation of an accounting fraud case involving AIG, Gen Re executives became implicated. On March 15, 2005, the AIG board forced Greenberg to resign from his post as chairman and CEO after New York state regulators claimed that AIG had engaged in questionable transactions and improper accounting.[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-56) On February 9, 2006, AIG agreed to pay a $1.6 billion fine (equivalent to $2,555,298,778in 2025).[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-57) In 2010, the U.S. government agreed to a $92 million (equivalent to $135,830,917in 2025) settlement with Gen Re, allowing the Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary to avoid prosecution in the AIG case. Gen Re also made a commitment to implement "corporate governance concessions", which required Berkshire Hathaway's chief financial officer to attend General Re's audit committee meetings and mandated the appointment of an independent director.[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Walk-54) In 2002, Buffett entered in $11 billion (equivalent to $19,690,101,758in 2025) worth of [forward contracts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forward_contract "Forward contract") to deliver U.S. dollars against other currencies. By April 2006, his total gain on these contracts was over $2 billion (equivalent to $17,567,679,102in 2025). Buffett announced in June 2006 that he would gradually give away 85% of his Berkshire holdings to five foundations in annual gifts of stock, starting in July 2006—the largest contribution going to the [Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_and_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation").[\[58\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-58) In 2007, in a letter to shareholders, Buffett announced that he was looking for a younger successor, or perhaps successors, to run his investment business.[\[59\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-59) ### 2007–08 financial crisis Buffett ran into criticism during the [subprime mortgage crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis") of 2007 and 2008, part of the [Great Recession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") starting in 2007, that he had allocated capital too early resulting in suboptimal deals.[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-60) "Buy American. I am." he wrote for an opinion piece published in the [_New York Times_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times") in 2008.[\[61\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-61) Buffett called the downturn in the financial sector that started in 2007 " [poetic justice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poetic_justice "Poetic justice")".[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-62) Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway suffered a 77% drop in earnings during Q3 2008 and several of his later deals suffered large [mark-to-market](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mark-to-market "Mark-to-market") losses.[\[63\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-63) On September 23, 2008, Berkshire Hathaway acquired 10 percent of perpetual [preferred stock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") of [Goldman Sachs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs").[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-64) Some of Buffett's put options (European exercise at expiry only) that he wrote (sold) were running at around $6.73 (equivalent to $10,063,794,436in 2025) billion mark-to-market losses as of late 2008.[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-reuters.com-65) The scale of the potential loss prompted the SEC to demand that Berkshire produce, "a more robust disclosure" of factors used to value the contracts.[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-reuters.com-65) Buffett also helped [Dow Chemical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dow_Chemical "Dow Chemical") pay for its $18.8 billion (equivalent to $28,112,828,439in 2025) takeover of [Rohm & Haas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rohm_&_Haas "Rohm & Haas"). He thus became the single largest shareholder in the enlarged group with his Berkshire Hathaway, which provided $3 billion (equivalent to $4,486,089,645in 2025), underlining his instrumental role during the crisis in debt and equity markets.[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-66) In 2008, Buffett became the richest person in the world, garnering a total net worth estimated at $62 billion (equivalent to $92,712,519,320in 2025)[\[67\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-67) by [Forbes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forbes "Forbes") and at $58 billion (equivalent to $86,731,066,461in 2025)[\[68\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-68) by [Yahoo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yahoo "Yahoo"), dethroning [Bill Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates"), who had been number one on the [Forbes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forbes_list_of_billionaires "Forbes list of billionaires") list for 13 consecutive years.[\[69\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-69) In 2009, Gates regained the top position on the Forbes list, with Buffett shifted to second place. Both of the men's values dropped, to $40 billion (equivalent to $60,027,919,963in 2025) and $37 billion (equivalent to $55,525,825,966in 2025) respectively—according to Forbes, Buffett lost $25 billion (equivalent to $37,517,449,977in 2025) over a 12-month period during 2008/2009.[\[70\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-70) In October 2008, the media reported that Buffett had agreed to buy [General Electric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") (GE) preferred stock.[\[71\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-71) The operation included special incentives: he received an option to buy three billion shares of GE stock, at $22.25 (equivalent to $33in 2025), over the five years following the agreement, and Buffett also received a 10% dividend (callable within three years). In February 2009, Buffett sold some Procter & Gamble Co. and Johnson & Johnson shares from his personal portfolio.[\[72\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-72) In addition to suggestions of mistiming, the wisdom in keeping some of Berkshire's major holdings, including The Coca-Cola Company, which in 1998 peaked at $86 (equivalent to $170in 2025), raised questions. Buffett discussed the difficulties of knowing when to sell in the company's 2004 annual report: > That may seem easy to do when one looks through an always-clean, rear-view mirror. Unfortunately, however, it's the windshield through which investors must peer, and that glass is invariably fogged.[\[73\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-73) In March 2009, Buffett said in a cable television interview that the economy had "fallen off a cliff ... Not only has the economy slowed down a lot, but people have really changed their habits like I haven't seen". Additionally, Buffett feared that inflation levels that occurred in the 1970s—which led to years of painful [stagflation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stagflation "Stagflation")—might re-emerge.[\[74\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-74)[\[75\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-75) ### A capitalized Berkshire In 2009, Buffett invested $2.6 billion (equivalent to $3,901,814,798in 2025) as a part of [Swiss Re](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Swiss_Re "Swiss Re")'s campaign to raise equity capital.[\[76\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-76)[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-77) Berkshire Hathaway already owned a 3% stake, with rights to own more than 20%.[\[78\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-78) Also in 2009, Buffett acquired [Burlington Northern Santa Fe Corp.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Burlington_Northern_Santa_Fe_Corp. "Burlington Northern Santa Fe Corp.") for $34 billion (equivalent to $51,023,731,968in 2025) in cash and stock. [Alice Schroeder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alice_Schroeder "Alice Schroeder"), author of _Snowball_, said that a key reason for the purchase was to diversify Berkshire Hathaway from the financial industry.[\[79\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-79) Measured by [market capitalization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") in the [Financial Times Global 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Financial_Times_Global_500 "Financial Times Global 500"), Berkshire Hathaway was the eighteenth largest corporation in the world as of June 2009.[\[80\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-80) In 2009, Buffett divested his failed investment in [ConocoPhillips](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ConocoPhillips "ConocoPhillips"), saying to his Berkshire investors: > I bought a large amount of ConocoPhillips stock when oil and gas prices were near their peak. I in no way anticipated the dramatic fall in energy prices that occurred in the last half of the year. I still believe the odds are good that oil sells far higher in the future than the current $40–$50 price. But so far I have been dead wrong. Even if prices should rise, moreover, the terrible timing of my purchase has cost Berkshire several billion dollars.[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-BuffettCOPBlunder-81) The merger with the Burlington Northern Santa Fe Railway ( [BNSF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BNSF "BNSF")) closed upon BNSF shareholder approval during Q1 of 2010. This deal was valued at approximately $44 billion, with $10 billion of outstanding BNSF debt (equivalent to $79,726,842,667in 2025) and represented an increase of the previously existing stake of 22%.[\[82\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-82)[\[83\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-83) In June 2010, Buffett defended the credit-rating agencies for their role in the U.S. financial crisis, claiming: > Very, very few people could appreciate the [bubble](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic_bubble "Economic bubble"). That's the nature of bubbles—they're mass delusions.[\[84\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-84) On March 18, 2011, [Goldman Sachs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") was given [Federal Reserve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") approval to buy back Berkshire's preferred stock in Goldman. Buffett had been reluctant to give up the stock, which averaged $1.4 million (equivalent to $2,003,698in 2025) in dividends per day,[\[85\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-85)[\[86\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-86) saying: > I'm going to be the [Osama bin Laden](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Osama_bin_Laden "Osama bin Laden") of capitalism. I'm on my way to an unknown destination in Asia where I'm going to look for a cave. If the U.S. Armed forces can't find Osama bin Laden in 10 years, let Goldman Sachs try to find me.[\[87\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-87) In November 2011, it was announced that over the course of the previous eight months, Buffett had bought 64million shares of [International Business Machine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Business_Machine "International Business Machine") Corp (IBM) stock, worth around $11 billion (equivalent to $15,743,343,195in 2025). This unanticipated investment raised his stake in the company to around 5.5 percent—the largest stake in IBM alongside that of [State Street Global Advisors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/State_Street_Global_Advisors "State Street Global Advisors"). Buffett had said on numerous prior occasions that he would not invest in technology because he did not fully understand it, so the move came as a surprise to many investors and observers. During the interview, in which he revealed the investment to the public, Buffett stated that he was impressed by the company's ability to retain corporate clients and said, "I don't know of any large company that really has been as specific on what they intend to do and how they intend to do it as IBM".[\[88\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-88) In May 2012, Buffett's acquisition of Media General, consisting of 63 newspapers in the south-eastern U.S., was announced.[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-89) The company was the second news print purchase made by Buffett in one year.[\[90\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-90) Interim publisher James W. Hopson announced on July 18, 2013, that the _[Press of Atlantic City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Press_of_Atlantic_City "Press of Atlantic City")_ would be sold to Buffett's BH Media Group by ABARTA, a private holding company based in [Pittsburgh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), U.S. At the Berkshire shareholders meeting in May 2013, Buffett explained that he did not expect to "move the needle" at Berkshire with newspaper acquisitions, but he anticipates an annual return of 10 percent. The _Press of Atlantic City_ became Berkshire's 30th daily newspaper, following other purchases such as Virginia, U.S.' _Roanoke Times_ and _The Tulsa World_ in Oklahoma, U.S.[\[91\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-91) During a presentation to [Georgetown University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University") students in [Washington, D.C.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), in late September 2013, Buffett compared the U.S. Federal Reserve to a hedge fund and stated that the bank is generating "$80 billion or $90 billion a year probably" in revenue for the U.S. government. Buffett also advocated further on the issue of wealth equality in society: > We have learned to turn out lots of goods and services, but we haven't learned as well how to have everybody share in the bounty. The obligation of a society as prosperous as ours is to figure out how nobody gets left too far behind.[\[92\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-92) After the difficulties of the [economic crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), Buffett managed to bring its company back to its pre-recession standards: in Q2 2014, Berkshire Hathaway made $6.4 billion (equivalent to $8,703,964,015in 2025) in net profit, the most it had ever made in a three-month period.[\[93\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-93) On August 14, 2014, the price of Berkshire Hathaway's shares hit $200,000 a share (equivalent to $271,999in 2025) for the first time, capitalizing the company at $328 billion (equivalent to $446,078,155,749in 2025). While Buffett had given away much of his stock to charities by this time, he still held 321,000 shares worth $64.2 billion (equivalent to $87,311,639,022in 2025). On August 20, 2014, Berkshire Hathaway was fined $896,000 (equivalent to $1,218,555in 2025) for failing to report as required the December 9, 2013 purchase of shares in [USG Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USG_Corporation "USG Corporation").[\[94\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-94) A 2023 [ProPublica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ProPublica "ProPublica") article based on a leak of confidential IRS data alleged that Buffett had made equity trades in his personal portfolio involving companies that Berkshire Hathaway bought or sold during the same quarter or the quarter before, raising concerns about conflicts of interest. On three dates between 2009 and 2012, Buffett sold shares of Johnson and Johnson, Walmart, and Wells Fargo, with the sales totaling $80 million in value (equivalent to $120,055,840in 2025).[\[95\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-95)[\[96\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-96) Although Buffett has not commented, Berkshire Hathaway's Vice Chairman Charlie Munger dismissed the allegations, saying "I don't think there's the slightest chance that Warren Buffett is doing something that is deeply evil to make money for himself."[\[97\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-97) At Berkshire Hathaway's investor conference on May 3, 2025, Buffett requested that the board appoint [Greg Abel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Abel "Greg Abel") to succeed him as CEO of the company by the end of the year.[\[98\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-98)[\[99\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-99) On May 5, 2025, the company announced the appointment of Abel as president and CEO, effective January 1, 2026, with Buffett remaining chairman.[\[100\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-100)[\[101\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-101) ## Investment philosophy Buffett's writings include his annual reports and various articles. Buffett is recognized by communicators[\[102\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-102) as a great story-teller, as evidenced by his annual letters to shareholders. He has warned about the pernicious effects of inflation:[\[103\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-103) > The arithmetic makes it plain that inflation is a far more devastating tax than anything that has been enacted by our legislatures. The inflation tax has a fantastic ability to simply consume capital. It makes no difference to a widow with her savings in a 5 percent passbook account whether she pays 100 percent income tax on her interest income during a period of zero inflation, or pays no income taxes during years of 5 percent inflation. —Buffett, Fortune (1977) In his article, " [The Superinvestors of Graham-and-Doddsville](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Superinvestors_of_Graham-and-Doddsville "The Superinvestors of Graham-and-Doddsville")", Buffett rebutted the academic [efficient-market hypothesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Efficient-market_hypothesis "Efficient-market hypothesis"), that beating the [S&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S&P_500 "S&P 500") was "pure chance", by highlighting the results achieved by a number of students of the Graham and Dodd [value investing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_investing "Value investing") school of thought. In addition to himself, Buffett named [Walter J. Schloss](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walter_J._Schloss "Walter J. Schloss"), Tom Knapp, Ed Anderson ( [Tweedy, Browne LLC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tweedy,_Browne "Tweedy, Browne")), [William J. Ruane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_J._Ruane "William J. Ruane") ( [Sequoia Fund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sequoia_Fund "Sequoia Fund")), [Charlie Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Munger "Charlie Munger") (Buffett's partner at Berkshire), Rick Guerin (Pacific Partners Ltd.), and Stan Perlmeter (Perlmeter Investments).[\[104\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-104) In his November 1999 _[Fortune](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortune_(magazine) "Fortune (magazine)")_ article, he warned of investors' unrealistic expectations:[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Mr._Buffett_on_the_stock_market-105) > Let me summarize what I've been saying about the stock market: I think it's very hard to come up with a persuasive case that equities will over the next 17 years perform anything like—anything like—they've performed in the past 17. If I had to pick the most probable return, from appreciation and dividends combined, that investors in aggregate—repeat, aggregate—would earn in a world of constant interest rates, 2% inflation, and those ever hurtful frictional costs, it would be 6%! —Buffett, Fortune (1999) ### Index funds vis-à-vis active management See also: [Jeffrey Tarrant §Buffett bet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeffrey_Tarrant#Buffett_bet "Jeffrey Tarrant") Buffett has been a supporter of [index funds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Index_fund "Index fund") for people who are either not interested in managing their own money or do not have the time. Buffett is skeptical that active management can outperform the market in the long run, and has advised both individual and institutional investors to move their money to low-cost index funds that track broad, diversified stock market indices. Buffett said in one of his letters to shareholders that "when trillions of dollars are managed by Wall Streeters charging high fees, it will usually be the managers who reap outsized profits, not the clients".[\[106\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-guardian-106) In 2007, Buffett made a bet with numerous managers that a simple [S&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S&P_500 "S&P 500") index fund will outperform [hedge funds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") that charge exorbitant fees. By 2017, the index fund was outperforming every hedge fund that made the bet against Buffett.[\[106\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-guardian-106) ### Using investment banks Buffett has a long-standing aversion to using the services of [investment banks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Investment_bank "Investment bank") via Berkshire Hathaway.[\[107\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-107) This dynamic was also reported in [Barron's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barron's_(newspaper) "Barron's (newspaper)"),[\[108\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-108) [Insider](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Business_Insider "Business Insider"),[\[109\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-109) and [Seeking Alpha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seeking_Alpha "Seeking Alpha"),[\[110\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-110) among others. ### Investment style In his early years, Warren Buffett was influenced by [Benjamin Graham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Benjamin_Graham "Benjamin Graham") who was a [value investor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value_investing "Value investing") and is known as "the father of value investing". When studying under Graham in [Columbia Business School](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Columbia_Business_School "Columbia Business School"), Buffett adopted that style which was to find undervalued stocks below their [intrinsic value](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intrinsic_value_(finance) "Intrinsic value (finance)") known as "cigar butts". He believed in buying "durable competitive stocks" rather than just "cheap stocks" which he assessed by [brand loyalty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brand_loyalty "Brand loyalty"), strong consistent earnings, [competitive advantage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Competitive_advantage "Competitive advantage") and pricing power[\[111\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-111) in addition to holding it for long term.[\[112\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:2-112) He came up with rules such as 1. "Never lose money", 2. "Never forget rule number 1", believing in the importance of protecting capital.[\[112\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:2-112) He believed the ideal holding period for a stock was forever and he even said in a 1996 letter to Berkshire Hathaway shareholders "If you aren't willing to own a stock for ten years, don't even think about owning it for ten minutes".[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-113)[\[114\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-114) ## Personal life [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/82/Storm_Chasers_press_conference.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Storm_Chasers_press_conference.jpg) With [Gary Green](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gary_Green_(baseball_owner) "Gary Green (baseball owner)") in 2010 In 1949, Buffett developed a crush on a young woman whose boyfriend had a [ukulele](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ukulele "Ukulele"). In an attempt to compete, he bought one of the instruments and has been playing it ever since. Though the attempt to capture her attention was unsuccessful, his music interest became a key part of his becoming a part of [Susan Thompson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susan_Buffett "Susan Buffett")'s life, and led to their marriage. Buffett often plays the instrument at stockholder meetings and other opportunities. His love of the instrument led to the commissioning of two custom Dairy Queen ukuleles by Dave Talsma, one of which was auctioned for charity.[\[115\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-115) In 1952,[\[116\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-washingtonpost.com-116) Buffett married Susan at Dundee [Presbyterian Church](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"). The following year, they had their first child, [Susan Alice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susan_Alice_Buffett "Susan Alice Buffett"). She was followed by [Howard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_Graham_Buffett "Howard Graham Buffett") (b. 1954) and [Peter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Buffett "Peter Buffett") (b. 1958). The couple began living separately in 1977, although they remained married until Susan's death in July 2004. Their daughter Susan lives in Omaha, is a national board member of [Girls, Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Girls,_Inc. "Girls, Inc."), and performs charitable work through the Susan A. Buffett Foundation.[\[117\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-117) In 2006, on his 76th birthday, Buffett married his longtime companion, Astrid Menks, who was then 60 years old—she had lived with him since his wife's departure to San Francisco in 1977.[\[118\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-118)[\[119\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-119) Susan had arranged for the two to meet before she left Omaha to pursue her singing career. All three were close and [Christmas cards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christmas_card "Christmas card") to friends were signed "Warren, Susie and Astrid".[\[120\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-lowenstein-120) Susan briefly discussed this relationship in an interview on the _[Charlie Rose Show](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Rose_(talk_show) "Charlie Rose (talk show)")_ shortly before her death, in a rare glimpse into Buffett's personal life.[\[121\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-121) Buffett disowned his son Peter's adopted daughter, Nicole, in 2006 after she participated in the [Jamie Johnson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamie_Johnson_(filmmaker) "Jamie Johnson (filmmaker)") documentary _[The One Percent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_One_Percent_(film) "The One Percent (film)")_ about the growing economic inequality between the wealthy and the average citizen in the United States. Although his first wife referred to Nicole as one of her "adored grandchildren",[\[122\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-122) Buffett wrote Nicole a letter stating, "I have not emotionally or legally adopted you as a grandchild, nor have the rest of my family adopted you as a niece or a cousin".[\[123\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-123)[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-124)[\[125\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-125) By 2022, she and Buffett had reconciled.[\[126\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-126)[\[127\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-127) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Dundee_HD_Omaha_NE.JPG/500px-Dundee_HD_Omaha_NE.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dundee_HD_Omaha_NE.JPG) Buffett's home in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska") His 2006 annual salary was about $100,000 (equivalent to $159,706in 2025), which is small compared to senior [executive remuneration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Executive_compensation "Executive compensation") in comparable companies.[\[128\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-fool1-128) In 2008, he earned a total compensation of $175,000 (equivalent to $261,689in 2025), which included a base salary of just $100,000 (equivalent to $149,536in 2025).[\[129\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-129) In 1958, Buffett purchased a five-bedroom stucco house in [Omaha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), where he still lives, for US$31,500(equivalent to $351,514 in 2025).[\[130\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-130)[\[131\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-131) He also owned a vacation home in [Laguna Beach, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laguna_Beach,_California "Laguna Beach, California"),[\[132\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-132) which he purchased for $150,000 in 1971 (equivalent to $1,192,482in 2025). He sold it for $7.5 million (equivalent to $9,616,022in 2025) in 2018.[\[133\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-133) In 1989, after spending nearly $6.7million (equivalent to $17,402,013in 2025) of Berkshire's funds on a [private jet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_jet "Private jet"), Buffett named it "The Indefensible", later renamed "The Indispensable".[\[134\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-134) This act was at odds with his past condemnation of extravagant spending by other CEOs.[\[135\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-135) Buffett sold the jet prior to mid-1999 and has since usually flown with Berkshire's flight services businesses.[\[136\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-136) > Bridge is such a sensational game that I wouldn't mind being in jail if I had three cellmates who were decent players and who were willing to keep the game going twenty-four hours a day. —Buffett on bridge[\[137\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-owen20070917-137) Buffett is an avid [bridge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contract_bridge "Contract bridge") player, which he plays with Gates[\[138\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-138) and champion player Sharon Osberg; he is said to spend 12 hours a week playing the game.[\[139\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-139)[\[140\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-140) In 2006, he sponsored a bridge match for the [Buffett Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffett_Cup "Buffett Cup"). Modeled on the [Ryder Cup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ryder_Cup "Ryder Cup") in golf—held immediately before it in the same city—the teams are chosen by invitation, with a female team and five male teams provided by each country.[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-141) When he purchased the _Omaha Sun_ from [Stanford Lipsey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanford_Lipsey "Stanford Lipsey") in 1969, Buffett volunteered to become the first non-Jewish member of Omaha's Highland Club, to set an example of nondiscrimination.[\[142\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-142) According to Lipsey, at that time the [Omaha Country Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha_Country_Club "Omaha Country Club") did not take Jewish members, and the Highland Club had no non-Jewish members.[\[143\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-143) He is a dedicated, lifelong follower of [Nebraska football](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebraska_Cornhuskers_football "Nebraska Cornhuskers football"), and attends as many games as his schedule permits. He supported the hire of [Bo Pelini](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bo_Pelini "Bo Pelini"), following the [2007 season](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2007_Nebraska_Cornhuskers_football_team "2007 Nebraska Cornhuskers football team"), stating, "It was getting kind of desperate around here".[\[144\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-144) He watched the 2009 game against Oklahoma from the Nebraska sideline, after being named an honorary assistant coach.[\[145\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-145) Buffett was elected to the [American Philosophical Society](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society") in 2009.[\[146\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-146) Buffett worked with Christopher Webber on an animated series called " [Secret Millionaires Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secret_Millionaires_Club "Secret Millionaires Club")" with chief [Andy Heyward](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andy_Heyward "Andy Heyward") of [DiC Entertainment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DiC_Entertainment "DiC Entertainment"). The series features Buffett and Munger and teaches children healthy financial habits.[\[147\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-147)[\[148\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-148) Buffett was raised as a [Presbyterian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), but has since described himself as [agnostic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic").[\[149\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-149) In December 2006, it was reported that Buffett did not carry a mobile phone, did not have a computer at his desk, and drove his own automobile,[\[150\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-150) a [Cadillac DTS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cadillac_DTS "Cadillac DTS").[\[151\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-151) In contrast to that, at the 2018 Berkshire Hathaway's shareholder meeting, he stated he uses Google as his preferred search engine.[\[152\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-152) In 2013 he had an old Nokia flip phone and had sent one email in his entire life.[\[153\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-153) In February 2020, Buffett revealed in a CNBC interview that he had traded in his flip phone for an [iPhone 11](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IPhone_11 "IPhone 11").[\[154\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-154) Buffett reads five newspapers every day, beginning with the _[Omaha World Herald](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha_World_Herald "Omaha World Herald")_, which his company acquired in 2011. Buffett's speeches are known for mixing business discussions with humor. Each year, Buffett presides over Berkshire Hathaway's annual shareholder meeting in the [Qwest Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Qwest_Center "Qwest Center") in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), an event drawing over 20,000 visitors from both the United States and abroad, giving it the nickname "Woodstock of Capitalism". Berkshire's annual reports and letters to shareholders, prepared by Buffett, frequently receive coverage by the financial media. Buffett's writings are known for containing quotations from sources as varied as the [Bible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bible "Bible") and [Mae West](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mae_West "Mae West"),[\[155\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-155) as well as advice in a folksy, Midwestern style and numerous jokes. In April 2017, Buffett (an avid [Coca-Cola](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") drinker and shareholder in the company) agreed to have his likeness placed on Cherry Coke products in [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China "China"). Buffett was not compensated for this advertisement.[\[156\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-156)[\[157\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-157) Buffett is very distantly related to the 44th president of the United States, [Barack Obama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama").[\[158\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-158) Buffett was a longtime friend of singer-songwriter [Jimmy Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jimmy_Buffett "Jimmy Buffett") until Jimmy's death in September 2023, and they would often refer to one another as "Uncle Warren" and "Cousin Jimmy". The two took a DNA test which revealed no relation.[\[159\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-159) ### Health Buffett is a [teetotaler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teetotalism "Teetotalism").[\[160\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-160) On April 11, 2012, Buffett was diagnosed with stage I [prostate cancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostate_cancer "Prostate cancer") during a routine test.[\[161\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Buffett-161) He announced he would begin two months of daily radiation treatment from mid-July. In a letter to shareholders, Buffett said he felt "great—as if I were in my normal excellent health—and my energy level is 100 percent".[\[161\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Buffett-161) On September 15, 2012, Buffett announced that he had completed the full 44-day radiation treatment cycle, saying "it's a great day for me" and "I am so glad to say that's over".[\[162\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-162) ## Wealth [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Kathy_Ireland%2C_Warren_Buffett_and_Bill_Gates_at_the_2015_Berkshire_Hathaway_Shareholders_Meeting.jpg/500px-Kathy_Ireland%2C_Warren_Buffett_and_Bill_Gates_at_the_2015_Berkshire_Hathaway_Shareholders_Meeting.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Kathy_Ireland,_Warren_Buffett_and_Bill_Gates_at_the_2015_Berkshire_Hathaway_Shareholders_Meeting.jpg) Buffett, [Kathy Ireland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kathy_Ireland "Kathy Ireland") and [Bill Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") at the 2015 Berkshire Hathaway shareholders meeting In 2008, Buffett was ranked by _[Forbes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")_ as the richest person in the world with an estimated [net worth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_worth "Net worth") of approximately $62 billion (equivalent to $92,712,519,320in 2025).[\[163\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-163) In 2009, after donating billions of dollars to charity, he was ranked as the second richest man in the United States with a net worth of $37 billion (equivalent to $55,525,825,966in 2025)[\[164\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-164)[\[165\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-165) with only [Bill Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") ranked higher than Buffett. His net worth had risen to $58.5 billion (equivalent to $80,855,357,143in 2025) as of September 2013.[\[166\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-166) In 1999, Buffett was named the Top Money Manager of the Twentieth Century in a survey by the Carson Group, ahead of [Peter Lynch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Lynch "Peter Lynch") and [John Templeton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Templeton "John Templeton").[\[167\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-findarticles.com-167) In 2007, he was listed among [_Time_'s 100 Most Influential People](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in the world.[\[168\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-168) In 2011, President [Barack Obama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") awarded him the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom").[\[169\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-169) Buffett, along with [Bill Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates"), was named the most influential global thinker in _[Foreign Policy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foreign_Policy "Foreign Policy")'_ s 2010 report.[\[170\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-170) Buffett has written several times of his belief that, in a market economy, the rich earn outsized rewards for their talents.[\[171\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-171) His children will not inherit a significant proportion of his wealth. He once commented, "I want to give my kids just enough so that they would feel that they could do anything, but not so much that they would feel like doing nothing".[\[172\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-172) ## Philanthropy Buffett had long stated his intention to give away his fortune to [charity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization"), and in June 2006, he announced a new plan to give 83% of it to the [Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_&_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation") (BMGF).[\[173\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-173) He pledged about the equivalent of 10 million [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") Class B shares to the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation (equivalent to $49,029,795,312in 2025),[\[174\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-174) making it the largest charitable donation in history, and Buffett one of the leaders of [philanthrocapitalism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philanthrocapitalism "Philanthrocapitalism").[\[175\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-175) The foundation will receive 5% of the total each July, beginning in 2006. The pledge is conditional upon three requirements: - Bill or Melinda Gates must be alive and active in BMGF - BMGF must continue to qualify as a charity - Each year BMGF must give away an amount equal to the prior year's Berkshire gift plus the additional 5% of net assets as required of all US foundations Buffett joined the Gates Foundation's board, but did not plan to be actively involved in the foundation's investments.[\[176\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-176)[\[177\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-177) Buffett announced his resignation as a trustee of the Gates Foundation on June 23, 2021.[\[178\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-178) This represented a significant shift from Buffett's previous statements, to the effect that most of his fortune would pass to his [Buffett Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffett_Foundation "Buffett Foundation").[\[179\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-179) The bulk of the estate of his wife, valued at $2.6 billion (equivalent to $4,431,818,182in 2025), went there when she died in 2004.[\[180\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-180) He also pledged $50 million (equivalent to $89,500,463in 2025) to the [Nuclear Threat Initiative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_Threat_Initiative "Nuclear Threat Initiative"), in Washington, where he began serving as an adviser in 2002.[\[181\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-181) In 2006, he auctioned his 2001 Lincoln Town Car on [eBay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EBay "EBay") to raise money for [Girls, Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Girls,_Inc. "Girls, Inc.")[\[182\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-182) In 2007, he auctioned a luncheon with himself that raised a final bid of $650,100 (equivalent to $1,009,423in 2025) for the [Glide Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glide_Foundation "Glide Foundation").[\[183\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-183) Later auctions raised $2.1 million (equivalent to $3,155,216in 2025),[\[184\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-184)[\[185\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-185) $1.7 million (equivalent to $2,509,919in 2025)[\[186\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-186) and $3.5 million (equivalent to $4,908,320in 2025).[\[187\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:3-187) The winners traditionally dine with Buffett at New York's [Smith and Wollensky steak house](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Smith_&_Wollensky "Smith & Wollensky"). The restaurant donates at least $10,000 to Glide each year to host the meal.[\[187\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:3-187) In 2009, Ralph Nader wrote the book _Only the Super Rich Can Save Us_, a novel about "a movement of billionaires led by Warren Buffett and featuring, among others, Ted Turner, George Soros and Barry Diller, who use their fortunes to clean up America". On C-SPAN BookTV, Nader said Buffett invited him to breakfast after the book came out and was "quite intrigued by the book". He also told Nader of his plan to get "billionaires all over the world to donate 50% of their estate to charity or good works".[\[188\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-188) On December 9, 2010, Buffett, Bill Gates, and [Facebook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") CEO [Mark Zuckerberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mark_Zuckerberg "Mark Zuckerberg") signed a promise they called the "Gates-Buffett [Giving Pledge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giving_Pledge "Giving Pledge")", in which they promise to donate to charity at least half of their wealth, and invite other wealthy people to follow suit.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-bbc-10)[\[189\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-189) In 2018, after making almost $3.4 billion donations,[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-190) Buffett was ranked 3rd in the _[Forbes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")_' List of Billionaires 2018.[\[191\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-191) Buffett continues to help fund and support his family's individual foundations which include [Susan Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susan_Buffett "Susan Buffett")'s _[Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffett_Foundation "Buffett Foundation")_, [Susan Alice Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susan_Alice_Buffett "Susan Alice Buffett")'s _Sherwood Foundation_, [Howard Graham Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_Graham_Buffett "Howard Graham Buffett")'s _Howard G. Buffett Foundation_, and [Peter Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Buffett "Peter Buffett")'s _NoVo Foundation_.[\[192\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-192)[\[193\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-193) Warren Buffett was also supportive of his sister [Doris Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doris_Buffett "Doris Buffett")'s _Letters Foundation_ and _Learning By Giving Foundation_.[\[194\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-194)[\[195\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-195) In November 2022, Buffett made a donation of $750 million (equivalent to $825,136,457in 2025) in [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") shares to four charitable foundations run by his children. 1.5 million [Class B shares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_B_share "Class B share") of his conglomerate to the Susan Thompson Buffett Foundation, named after his first wife. He also transferred 300,000 Class B shares each to three funds managed by his children: the Sherwood Foundation, the Howard G. Buffett Foundation and the NoVo Foundation.[\[196\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-196)As of 2023[\[update\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Warren_Buffett&action=edit), Buffett has given over $50 billion to charitable causes (equivalent to $52,834,207,077in 2025).[\[197\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-197)[\[198\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-198) In June 2025, Buffett donated $6 billion in [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") shares to five [charitable foundations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foundation_(nonprofit) "Foundation (nonprofit)"). A majority of shares were donated to the [Gates Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gates_Foundation "Gates Foundation") with the rest received by four other charitable foundations run by their children. As of June 2025, Buffett had donated over $60 billion to charitable causes.[\[199\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-199)[\[200\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-200)[\[201\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-201) ## Political and public policy views [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/President_Barack_Obama_and_Warren_Buffett_in_the_Oval_Office%2C_July_14%2C_2010.jpg/500px-President_Barack_Obama_and_Warren_Buffett_in_the_Oval_Office%2C_July_14%2C_2010.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:President_Barack_Obama_and_Warren_Buffett_in_the_Oval_Office,_July_14,_2010.jpg) Buffett and President Obama in the [Oval Office](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office"), July 14, 2010 In addition to political contributions over the years, Buffett endorsed and made campaign contributions to [Barack Obama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s [presidential campaign](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama_2008_presidential_campaign "Barack Obama 2008 presidential campaign"). On July 2, 2008, Buffett attended a $28,500 (equivalent to $42,618in 2025) per plate fundraiser for Obama's campaign in Chicago.[\[202\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-202) Buffett intimated that [John McCain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain")'s views on [social justice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") were so far from his own that McCain would need a " [lobotomy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lobotomy "Lobotomy")" for Buffett to change his endorsement.[\[203\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-203) During the second [2008 U.S. presidential debate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election_debates "2008 United States presidential election debates"), McCain and Obama, after being asked first by presidential debate mediator [Tom Brokaw](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tom_Brokaw "Tom Brokaw"), both mentioned Buffett as a possible future [Secretary of the Treasury](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secretary_of_the_Treasury "Secretary of the Treasury").[\[204\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-204) Later, in the third and final presidential debate, Obama mentioned Buffett as a potential economic advisor.[\[205\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-205) Buffett was also a financial advisor to Republican candidate [Arnold Schwarzenegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") during the [2003 California gubernatorial election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2003_California_gubernatorial_election "2003 California gubernatorial election").[\[206\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-206) On December 16, 2015, Buffett endorsed Democratic candidate [Hillary Clinton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") for president.[\[207\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-auto-207) On August 1, 2016, Buffett challenged [Donald Trump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") to release his tax returns.[\[208\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-208)[\[209\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-209) On October 10, 2016, after a reference to him in [the second presidential debate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_debates "2016 United States presidential election debates"), Buffett released his own tax return.[\[210\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-210)[\[211\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:0-211) He said he had paid $1.85 million (equivalent to $2,512,811in 2025) in federal income taxes in 2015 on an adjusted gross income of $11.6 million (equivalent to $15,756,002in 2025), meaning he had an effective federal income tax rate of around 16 percent. Buffett also said he had made more than $2.8 billion worth of donations in the prior year (equivalent to $3,807,984,256in 2025).[\[211\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:0-211) In response to Trump saying he was unable to release his tax information due to being under audit, Buffett said, "I have been audited by the IRS multiple times and am currently being audited. I have no problem in releasing my tax information while under audit.
