Neither would Mr. Trump—at least he would have no legal problem."[\[211\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-:0-211) Buffett has said he would judge President Donald Trump by his results on national safety, economic growth and economic participation when deciding if he would vote for him in the [2020 presidential election](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[\[212\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-212)[\[213\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-213) ### Health care Buffett described the [health care reform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_care_reform_in_the_United_States "Health care reform in the United States") under President Barack Obama as insufficient to deal with the costs of health care in the U.S., though he supports its aim of expanding [health insurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance") coverage.[\[214\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Six-214) Buffett compared health care costs to a [tapeworm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tapeworm "Tapeworm"), saying that they compromise US economic competitiveness by increasing manufacturing costs.[\[214\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Six-214) Buffett said in 2010 that it was not sustainable for the U.S. to [devote 17% of its GDP to healthcare expenditure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_health_expenditure_per_capita "List of countries by total health expenditure per capita"), noting that many other nations spent a much smaller proportion of their GDP on health expenditures, with better healthcare outcomes.[\[215\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-215) Buffett said, "If you want the very best, I mean if you want to spend a million dollars to prolong your life 3 months in a coma or something then the US is probably the best", but he also said that other countries spend much less and receive much more in health care value (visits, hospital beds, doctors and nurses per capita).[\[216\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-216) Buffett faults the [incentives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Incentive "Incentive") in the United States [medical industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_industry "Medical industry"), that payers reimburse doctors for procedures ( [fee-for-service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fee-for-service "Fee-for-service")) leading to unnecessary care ( [overutilization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overutilization "Overutilization")), instead of paying for results.[\[217\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Nine-217) He cited [Atul Gawande](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atul_Gawande "Atul Gawande")'s 2009 article in the _New Yorker_[\[218\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Conundrum-218) as a useful consideration of U.S. health care, with its documentation of [unwarranted variation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unwarranted_variation "Unwarranted variation") in Medicare expenditures between [McAllen, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/McAllen,_Texas "McAllen, Texas") and [El Paso, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas").[\[217\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Nine-217) Buffett raised the problem of lobbying by the medical industry, saying that they are very focused on maintaining their income.[\[219\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-219) ### Curbing population growth Buffett has expressed concerns about unchecked [population growth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth"). In 2009, he met with several other billionaires to discuss healthcare, education and slowing population growth. Called "The Good Club" by an insider, the billionaires had given away $45 billion (equivalent to $5,598,238,395in 2025) to [philanthropic causes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy") and included [Oprah Winfrey](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oprah_Winfrey "Oprah Winfrey"), [Michael Bloomberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") and [David Rockefeller, Jr.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Rockefeller,_Jr. "David Rockefeller, Jr.")[\[220\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-220) The meeting has drawn criticism from some right-wing blogs, with some believing the group to be a part of a secret [sterilization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Population_control "Population control") society.[\[221\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-221) Buffett is a long-time supporter of family planning. The [Buffett Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffett_Foundation "Buffett Foundation") has given over $1.5 billion (equivalent to $2,012,271,215in 2025) to abortion research to include $427 million (equivalent to $572,826,539in 2025) to [Planned Parenthood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planned_Parenthood "Planned Parenthood").[\[222\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-222) ### Taxes See also: [Buffett Rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffett_Rule "Buffett Rule") President Obama announcing the " [Buffett Rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffett_Rule "Buffett Rule")" Buffett stated that he only paid 19% of his income for 2006, or around $48.1 million (equivalent to $76,818,670in 2025) in total federal taxes (due to their source as dividends and capital gains) while his employees paid 33% of theirs, despite making much less money.[\[223\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-223) Regarding how little he pays in taxes compared to his employees, he said, "How can this be fair? How can this be right? There's class warfare, all right, but it's my class, the rich class, that's making war, and we're winning."[\[224\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Stein-224)[\[225\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-Zweifel-225) After [Donald Trump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") accused him of taking "massive deductions", Buffett countered, "I have copies of all 72 of my returns and none uses a [carryforward](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tax_Attractiveness_Index#Loss_carryforward "Tax Attractiveness Index")."[\[226\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-trump_comments-226) Buffett favors the [inheritance tax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inheritance_tax "Inheritance tax"), saying that repealing it would be like "choosing the 2020 Olympic team by picking the eldest sons of the gold-medal winners in the 2000 Olympics".[\[227\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-interitance_tax-227) In 2007, Buffett testified before the Senate and urged them to preserve the [estate tax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Estate_tax_in_the_United_States "Estate tax in the United States") so as to avoid a [plutocracy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plutocracy "Plutocracy").[\[228\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-228) Some critics argued that Buffett (through Berkshire Hathaway) has a personal interest in the continuation of the estate tax, since Berkshire Hathaway benefited from the estate tax in past business dealings and had developed and marketed insurance policies to protect policy holders against future estate tax payments.[\[229\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-229) Buffett believes government should not be in the business of gambling, or legalizing [casinos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Casino "Casino"), calling it a tax on ignorance.[\[230\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-230) ### Dollar and gold The trade deficit induced Buffett to enter the foreign currency market for the first time in 2002. He substantially reduced his stake in 2005 as changing interest rates increased the costs of holding currency contracts. Buffett remained [bearish](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bear_market "Bear market") on the dollar, stating that he was looking to acquire companies with substantial foreign revenues. Buffett has been critical of [gold as an investment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gold_as_an_investment "Gold as an investment"), with his critique being based primarily on its non-productive nature. In a 1998 address at Harvard, Buffett said: > It gets dug out of the ground in Africa, or someplace. Then we melt it down, dig another hole, bury it again and pay people to stand around guarding it. It has no utility. Anyone watching from Mars would be scratching their head. In 1977, about stocks, gold, farmland and inflation, he stated: > Stocks are probably still the best of all the poor alternatives in an era of inflation—at least they are if you buy in at appropriate prices.[\[231\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-231) He has made a number of remarks about gold.[\[232\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-232) ### China Buffett invested in [PetroChina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PetroChina "PetroChina") Company Limited and in a rare move, posted a commentary[\[233\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-233) on Berkshire Hathaway's website stating why he would not divest over its connection with the [Sudanese civil war](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_Sudanese_Civil_War "Second Sudanese Civil War") that caused Harvard to divest. He sold this stake soon afterwards, sparing him the billions of dollars he would have lost had he held on to the company in the midst of the steep drop in oil prices beginning in the summer of 2008. In October 2008, Buffett invested $230 million for 10% of battery maker [BYD Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BYD_Company "BYD Company") ( [SEHK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stock_Exchange "Hong Kong Stock Exchange"): [1211](https://www.hkex.com.hk/Market-Data/Securities-Prices/Equities/Equities-Quote?sym=1211&sc_lang=en)), which also operates [an electric automobile manufacturing subsidiary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BYD_Auto "BYD Auto"). In less than one year, the investment reaped over 500% return.[\[234\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-234) ### Tobacco During the [RJR Nabisco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RJR_Nabisco "RJR Nabisco"), Inc., hostile takeover fight in 1987, Buffett was quoted as telling [John Gutfreund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Gutfreund "John Gutfreund"):[\[235\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-235) > I'll tell you why I like the cigarette business. It costs a penny to make. Sell it for a dollar. It's addictive. And there's fantastic [brand loyalty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brand_loyalty "Brand loyalty"). —Buffett, quoted in _Barbarians at the Gate: The Fall of RJR Nabisco_ Speaking at Berkshire Hathaway Inc.'s 1994 annual meeting, Buffett said investments in tobacco are:[\[236\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-236) > fraught with questions that relate to societal attitudes and those of the present administration. I would not like to have a significant percentage of my net worth invested in tobacco businesses. The economy of the business may be fine, but that doesn't mean it has a bright future. —Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway annual meeting ### Coal In 2007, Buffett's [PacifiCorp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PacifiCorp "PacifiCorp"), a subsidiary of his [MidAmerican Energy Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MidAmerican_Energy_Company "MidAmerican Energy Company"), canceled six proposed coal-fired power plants. These included Utah's [Intermountain Power Project](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intermountain_Power_Project "Intermountain Power Project") Unit 3, Jim Bridger Unit 5, and four proposed plants previously included in PacifiCorp's Integrated Resource Plan. The cancellations came in the wake of pressure from regulators and citizen groups.[\[237\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-237) ### Renewable energy Native American tribes and salmon fishermen sought to win support from Buffett for a proposal to remove four hydroelectric dams from the [Klamath River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Klamath_River "Klamath River") owned by [PacifiCorp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PacifiCorp "PacifiCorp") which is a [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") company. David Sokol responded on Buffett's behalf, stating that the [FERC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FERC "FERC") would decide the question.[\[238\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-238)[\[239\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-239) ### Expensing of stock options He has been a strong proponent of [stock option expensing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stock_option_expensing "Stock option expensing") on corporate [income statements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Income_statement "Income statement"). At the 2004 annual meeting, he lambasted a bill before the United States Congress that would consider only some company-issued stock options compensation as an expense, likening the bill to one that was almost passed by the [Indiana House of Representatives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indiana_House_of_Representatives "Indiana House of Representatives") to [change the value of Pi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Indiana_Pi_Bill "Indiana Pi Bill") from 3.14159 to 3.2 through legislative fiat.[\[240\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-240) > When a company gives something of value to its employees in return for their services, it is clearly a compensation expense. And if expenses don't belong in the earnings statement, where in the world do they belong?[\[241\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-241) ### High technology In May 2012, Buffett said he had avoided buying stock in [high-technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-technology "High-technology") companies such as [Facebook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms") and [Google](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google "Google") due to them being complex and difficult to understand on top of their concomitant complications from being hard to confidently estimate their future value. He also stated that [initial public offering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering")'s (IPO) of new stock issues are almost always bad investments. Buffett has advised investors to look for companies that will have good value in ten years.[\[242\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-242) ### Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies In an interview with [CNBC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNBC "CNBC") in January 2018, Buffett said the recent craze over [Bitcoin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") and other [cryptocurrencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cryptocurrencies "Cryptocurrencies") would not end well, adding that "when it happens or how or anything else, I don't know;" and later that year calling it, "rat poison squared." But he also said he would not take a short position on bitcoin futures.[\[243\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-243) ### COVID-19 pandemic In a June 2021 interview with [CNBC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNBC "CNBC"), Buffet said that the economic impact of the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") has increased [economic inequality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") and bemoaned that most people are unaware that "hundreds of thousands or millions" of small businesses have been negatively impacted. He also stated that the markets and the economy will likely be unpredictable well into the post-pandemic recovery period, even with the [Biden administration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Presidency of Joe Biden") and the United States [Federal Reserve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") having a plan in place. He said the unpredictability and the effects of COVID-19 are far from over.[\[244\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-244) ## Film and television Aside from countless television appearances on various news programs ( [Adam Smith](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Goodman "George Goodman")'s Money World in 1985 reportedly being the first),[\[245\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-245) Buffett has appeared in numerous films and TV programs, both documentary, and fiction. Some film and television cameos he has made include _[Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall_Street:_Money_Never_Sleeps "Wall Street: Money Never Sleeps")_ (2010), _[The Office](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Office_(American_TV_series) "The Office (American TV series)")_ (U.S.), _[All My Children](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/All_My_Children "All My Children"),_ and _[Entourage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entourage_(film) "Entourage (film)")_ (2015).[\[246\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-246) He has been a guest 10 times on [_Charlie Rose_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Rose_(talk_show) "Charlie Rose (talk show)"), and was the subject of the [HBO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HBO "HBO") documentary feature _[Becoming Warren Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Becoming_Warren_Buffett "Becoming Warren Buffett")_ (2017) and the [BBC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BBC "BBC") production _The World's Greatest Money Maker_ (2009). ## Bibliography [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/2018_Berkshire_Sunday_at_Borsheims_-_41039852065.jpg/500px-2018_Berkshire_Sunday_at_Borsheims_-_41039852065.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:2018_Berkshire_Sunday_at_Borsheims_-_41039852065.jpg) Buffett playing cards with [Bill Gates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") at [Borsheims](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Borsheims "Borsheims") during the 2018 shareholder's weekend ### Books about Buffett In October 2008, _[USA Today](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")_ reported at least 47 books were in print with Buffett's name in the title. The article quoted the CEO of [Borders Books](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Borders_Books "Borders Books"), George Jones, as saying that the only other living persons named in as many book titles were U.S. presidents, world political figures and the [Dalai Lama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/14th_Dalai_Lama "14th Dalai Lama").[\[247\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-name_game-247) Buffett said that his own personal favorite is a collection of his essays called _The Essays of Warren Buffett_,[\[248\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-248) which he described as "a coherent rearrangement of ideas from my annual report letters".[\[247\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett#cite_note-name_game-247) Books or publications by Buffett: - _The Essays of Warren Buffett: Lessons for Corporate America_, Warren Buffett and [Lawrence A. Cunningham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lawrence_A._Cunningham "Lawrence A. Cunningham"), The Cunningham Group; revised edition (April 11, 2001). [ISBN](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-9664461-1-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-9664461-1-1 "Special:BookSources/978-0-9664461-1-1"). - _The Essays of Warren Buffett: Lessons for Corporate America, Second Edition_, Warren E. Buffett and Lawrence A. Cunningham, The Cunningham Group; 2nd edition (April 14, 2008). [\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-3)[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-10K-4)[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14A-5) | Berkshire Hathaway Inc.[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Peter_Hilal%2C_Warren_Buffett%2C_Paul_Hilal%2C_Charlie_Munger.jpg/500px-Peter_Hilal%2C_Warren_Buffett%2C_Paul_Hilal%2C_Charlie_Munger.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Peter_Hilal,_Warren_Buffett,_Paul_Hilal,_Charlie_Munger.jpg) Buffett (second from left) and Munger (at right) in 1998 **Berkshire Hathaway Inc.** ([/ˈbɜːrkʃər/](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is an American [multinational](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") [conglomerate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conglomerate_(company) "Conglomerate (company)") [holding company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holding_company "Holding company") headquartered in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"). Originally a textile manufacturer, the company transitioned into a [conglomerate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conglomerate_(company) "Conglomerate (company)") starting in 1965 under the management of chairman and CEO [Warren Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett "Warren Buffett") (from 1970 to 2025) and vice chairman [Charlie Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Munger "Charlie Munger") (from 1978 to 2023). [Greg Abel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Abel "Greg Abel") succeeded Buffett as CEO at the beginning of 2026.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-Abel-CEO-6) Buffett personally owns 38.4% of the Class A voting shares of Berkshire Hathaway, representing a 15.1% overall economic interest in the company.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14A-5) The company is often compared to an [investment fund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Investment_fund "Investment fund"); between 1965, when Buffett gained control of the company, and 2023, the company's shareholder returns amounted to a [compound annual growth rate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compound_annual_growth_rate "Compound annual growth rate") (CAGR) of 19.8% compared to a 10.2% CAGR for the [S&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S&P_500 "S&P 500").[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-:0-7) From 1965 to 2023, the stock price had negative performance in only eleven years.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-:2-8) In August 2024, Berkshire Hathaway became the eighth largest U.S. [public company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_company "Public company") and the first non-technology company to be valued at over $1 trillion.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-:3-9) Berkshire Hathaway is ranked 5th on the [_Fortune_ 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") and 9th on the [_Fortune_ Global 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500").[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-:4-10) In surveys taken in the 2000s and 2010s by _[Barron's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barron's "Barron's")_, Berkshire was named among the "Most Respected" companies by professional investors.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-11)[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-12)[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-13)[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14) Berkshire is one of the ten largest components of the [S&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S&P_500 "S&P 500")[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-15) and is on the [list of largest employers in the United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_largest_employers_in_the_United_States "List of largest employers in the United States"). Its [class A shares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_A_share "Class A share") have the highest per-share price of any public company in the world, reaching $700,000 in August 2024, because the [board of directors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors") has historically been opposed to [stock splits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stock_split "Stock split").[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-16) ## History Berkshire Hathaway originated as a textile manufacturer formed in 1955 from the merger of [Hathaway Manufacturing Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hathaway_Mills "Hathaway Mills") and [Berkshire Fine Spinning Associates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Fine_Spinning_Associates "Berkshire Fine Spinning Associates"). Berkshire Associates took its name from [Berkshire County, Massachusetts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_County,_Massachusetts "Berkshire County, Massachusetts"), where it was based, and Hathaway Manufacturing was named for its founder, [Horatio Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horatio_Hathaway "Horatio Hathaway").[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-17)[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-18)[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-19)[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-20)[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-Grow-21) After the merger, Berkshire Hathaway had 15 plants employing over 12,000 workers with over $120million in revenue, and was headquartered in [New Bedford](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Bedford,_Massachusetts "New Bedford, Massachusetts").[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-Grow-21) However, seven of those locations were closed by the end of the 1950s, accompanied by large layoffs. In 1962, Warren Buffett began buying Berkshire Hathaway stock for his [investment fund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Investment_fund "Investment fund"), Buffett Partnership Ltd., at $7.50 per share, anticipating that as the company liquidated textile mills there would come a [tender offer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tender_offer "Tender offer") when he could sell the shares at a profit. A year later, Buffett's fund owned 7% of the company.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-stupid-22) In 1964, Buffett offered to sell his shares back to the company for $11.50 each.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-stupid-22) [Seabury Stanton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seabury_Stanton "Seabury Stanton"), the manager of Berkshire Hathaway, told Buffett orally that he had a deal.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-stupid-22) A few weeks later, Warren Buffett received the tender offer in writing, but the tender offer was for only $11.375 per share. Buffett later admitted that this lower, undercutting offer made him angry.[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-Worst-23) Instead of selling at the slightly lower price, Buffett bought more of the stock at an even higher price to take control of the company and fire Stanton; Stanton and his son resigned in 1965.[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-24) However, this left Buffett's fund with a major interest in a declining textile business. Buffett has described buying the Berkshire Hathaway textile company as the biggest investment mistake he had ever made, denying him compounded investment returns of about $200billion over the subsequent 45 years.[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-Worst-23) He has estimated that had he invested the same money directly in insurance businesses instead of indirectly via Berkshire Hathaway (due to what he perceived as a slight by an individual), it would have paid off several hundredfold.[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-25) In 1985, the company's last textile operations were shut down.[\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-26) Also in 1962, Warren Buffett set up his office in Omaha, Nebraska, at the current Berkshire Hathaway headquarters located at [Blackstone Plaza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blackstone_Plaza "Blackstone Plaza") (formerly named Kiewit Plaza).[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-27)[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-28) As of the end of 2024, the company had only 27 employees at that corporate headquarters.[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-29) In 1963, [Franklin Otis Booth Jr.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franklin_Otis_Booth_Jr. "Franklin Otis Booth Jr.") invested $1 million in the company (equivalent to $10,500,000in 2025), and in 1968 [David Gottesman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Gottesman "David Gottesman") also invested in the company. The investments eventually made both men billionaires.[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-30) ### Acquisitions See also: [List of assets owned by Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_assets_owned_by_Berkshire_Hathaway "List of assets owned by Berkshire Hathaway") In March 1967, Berkshire acquired National Indemnity Company, based in [Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), for $8.6 million in the first acquisition by the company under Buffett's control and the company's entrance into the insurance business. National Indemnity was founded in 1940 by Jack Dabney Ringwalt.[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-31) In 2012, National Indemnity acquired [workers' compensation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Workers'_compensation "Workers' compensation") insurer GUARD for $221 million.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-32) Berkshire acquired [See's Candies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/See's_Candies "See's Candies") in 1972 for $25 million.[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-reasons-33) It produces boxed chocolates and other confectionery products in two large kitchens in California. See's revenues are highly seasonal, with approximately 50% of total annual revenue earned in the months of November and December.[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-34) By 2019, the $25 million investment had returned over $2 billion, or an 8000% return, to Berkshire.[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-reasons-33) In 1976, Berkshire first invested in [GEICO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEICO "GEICO"), at a time when it was facing financial difficulty and had a very low stock price.[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-35) In 1977, Berkshire bought the _[Buffalo Evening News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffalo_Evening_News "Buffalo Evening News")_ and resumed publication of a Sunday edition of the paper that had ceased in 1914. After the morning newspaper _[Buffalo Courier-Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buffalo_Courier-Express "Buffalo Courier-Express")_ ceased operation in 1982, the _Buffalo Evening News_ changed its name to _The Buffalo News_ and began to print morning and evening editions. It now prints only a morning edition.[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-36) Berkshire formed BH Media Group with a purchase of the _[Omaha World-Herald](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha_World-Herald "Omaha World-Herald")_ in December 2011,[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-37) which included six other daily newspapers and several weeklies across Nebraska and southwest Iowa.[\[37\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-38) In June 2012, Berkshire bought 63 newspapers from [Media General](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Media_General "Media General"), including the _[Richmond Times-Dispatch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Richmond_Times-Dispatch "Richmond Times-Dispatch")_ and _[Winston-Salem Journal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winston-Salem_Journal "Winston-Salem Journal")_, for $142 million in cash.[\[38\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-39) In 2012, Berkshire Hathaway bought Texas dailies _[The Bryan-College Station Eagle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Bryan-College_Station_Eagle "The Bryan-College Station Eagle")_ and the _[Waco Tribune-Herald](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waco_Tribune-Herald "Waco Tribune-Herald")_.[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-40) In 2013, the company bought the _[Tulsa World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tulsa_World "Tulsa World")_, the [Greensboro, North Carolina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greensboro,_North_Carolina "Greensboro, North Carolina")-based _[News & Record](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/News_&_Record "News & Record")_, Virginia's _[Roanoke Times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roanoke_Times "Roanoke Times")_, and _[Press of Atlantic City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Press_of_Atlantic_City "Press of Atlantic City")_.[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-41) In 2014, [Graham Holdings Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graham_Holdings_Company "Graham Holdings Company") sold its Miami television station, [ABC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") affiliate [WPLG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WPLG "WPLG") to BH Media in a cash and stock deal.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-42)[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-43) In June 2018, Lee Enterprises and Berkshire Hathaway reached a five-year agreement to allow Lee Enterprises to manage Berkshire Hathaway's newspaper and digital operations.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-44) In 2020, [Lee Enterprises](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lee_Enterprises "Lee Enterprises") acquired BH Media Group's publications and _[The Buffalo News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Buffalo_News "The Buffalo News")_ for $140 million in cash, retaining [WPLG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WPLG "WPLG").[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-NO-45)[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-46) In conjunction with the sale, Berkshire lent $576 million to Lee.[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-NO-45) In 1983, Berkshire acquired [Nebraska Furniture Mart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebraska_Furniture_Mart "Nebraska Furniture Mart") for $60 million.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-47)[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-48) In 2000, Nebraska Furniture Mart bought Homemakers Furniture.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-49) In 1986, Berkshire acquired [Scott Fetzer Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scott_Fetzer_Company "Scott Fetzer Company"), a diversified group of 32 brands that manufactures and distributes products for residential, industrial, and institutional use, including [Ginsu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ginsu "Ginsu") knives and [World Book Encyclopedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Book_Encyclopedia "World Book Encyclopedia"). It included [Kirby Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kirby_Company "Kirby Company"), which was sold in 2021, Wayne Water Systems, and [Campbell Hausfeld](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Campbell_Hausfeld "Campbell Hausfeld") products.[\[49\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-50) Campbell Hausfeld was transferred to Marmon, also a Berkshire subsidiary, in 2015. In January 1986, Berkshire acquired Fechheimer Brothers, owner of Flying Cross, which manufactures public safety uniforms, and Vertx, a civilian tactical clothing company.[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-51) In 1991, Berkshire acquired [H.H. Brown Shoe Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H.H._Brown_Shoe_Group "H.H. Brown Shoe Group").[\[51\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-52) In May 1995, Berkshire acquired [RC Willey Home Furnishings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RC_Willey_Home_Furnishings "RC Willey Home Furnishings").[\[52\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-53) In June 1997, Berkshire acquired [Star Furniture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Star_Furniture "Star Furniture").[\[53\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-54) In January 1996, Berkshire acquired full ownership of [GEICO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEICO "GEICO"), acquiring the 49% of the company that it did not then own for $2.3 billion.[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-55) GEICO, headquartered in [Chevy Chase, Maryland](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chevy_Chase,_Maryland "Chevy Chase, Maryland"), primarily offers private passenger automobile insurance marketed directly to individual consumers in the United States.[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-56) The company is known for its advertising characters including a gecko and the [GEICO Cavemen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEICO_Cavemen "GEICO Cavemen"). [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/FlightSafety_International_C-17_Globemaster_III_full_flight_simulator_at_Joint_Base_Pearl_Harbor-Hickam.jpg/500px-FlightSafety_International_C-17_Globemaster_III_full_flight_simulator_at_Joint_Base_Pearl_Harbor-Hickam.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:FlightSafety_International_C-17_Globemaster_III_full_flight_simulator_at_Joint_Base_Pearl_Harbor-Hickam.jpg) [C-17 Globemaster III](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C-17_Globemaster_III "C-17 Globemaster III") full flight simulator at [Joint Base Pearl Harbor–Hickam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joint_Base_Pearl_Harbor%E2%80%93Hickam "Joint Base Pearl Harbor–Hickam") In February 1997, Berkshire acquired FlightSafety International (FSI), a [pilot training](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pilot_training "Pilot training") company founded in 1951 by [Albert Lee Ueltschi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Lee_Ueltschi "Albert Lee Ueltschi") and headquartered at [LaGuardia Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LaGuardia_Airport "LaGuardia Airport") in [Flushing, New York](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flushing,_New_York "Flushing, New York"). It uses [flight simulators](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flight_simulators "Flight simulators") and is the largest provider of professional aviation training services.[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-57)[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-58)[\[58\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-59)[\[59\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-60) In October 1997, Berkshire acquired [Dairy Queen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dairy_Queen "Dairy Queen"), based in Edina, Minnesota, for $585 million. It franchises approximately 6,000 stores operating under the names Dairy Queen, [Orange Julius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orange_Julius "Orange Julius"), and [Karmelkorn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karmelkorn "Karmelkorn") that sell dairy desserts, beverages, prepared foods, blended fruit drinks, popcorn, and other snack foods.[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-61)[\[61\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-62) In July 1998, Berkshire Hathaway acquired [NetJets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NetJets "NetJets"), formerly Executive Jet Aviation, for $725 million in cash.[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-63)[\[63\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-64) NetJets is the world's largest provider of [fractional ownership](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractional_ownership "Fractional ownership") programs for [general aviation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") aircraft. In 1986, NetJets created the fractional ownership of aircraft concept and introduced its NetJets program in the United States with one aircraft type. In 2019, the NetJets program operated more than 10 aircraft types with a fleet size of greater than 750.[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-65) In December 1998, Berkshire acquired [Gen Re](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gen_Re "Gen Re"), headquartered in [Stamford, Connecticut](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut"), for $22 billion.[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-66)[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-67) Berkshire paid for the acquisition with Class A shares valued at $58,400 each; Buffett later said he overpaid, despite strong performance by General Re.[\[67\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-68) [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/HomeServices_of_America_in_Gillette%2C_Wyoming.jpg/500px-HomeServices_of_America_in_Gillette%2C_Wyoming.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:HomeServices_of_America_in_Gillette,_Wyoming.jpg) A HomeServices of America in [Gillette, Wyoming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gillette,_Wyoming "Gillette, Wyoming") In 1999, Berkshire acquired MidAmerican Energy Holdings. At the time of purchase, Berkshire's [voting interest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voting_interest "Voting interest") was limited to 10% of the company's shares, but this restriction ended when the [Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_Utility_Holding_Company_Act_of_1935 "Public Utility Holding Company Act of 1935") was repealed in 2005. It owns [Northern Powergrid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northern_Powergrid "Northern Powergrid"), which operates in the UK.[\[68\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-69) In 2005, it acquired [PacifiCorp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PacifiCorp "PacifiCorp") for $5.1 billion in cash, and assumed $4.3 billion in PacificCorp debt and preferred stock.[\[69\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-70) MidAmerican was rebranded as [Berkshire Hathaway Energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway_Energy "Berkshire Hathaway Energy") (BHE) in April 2014.[\[70\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-71) The division owns a residential real estate brokerage business, which, in March 2013, was rebranded as [HomeServices of America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HomeServices_of_America "HomeServices of America"), a division of [Berkshire Hathaway Energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway_Energy "Berkshire Hathaway Energy").[\[71\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-72) In addition to brokerage services, it provides [mortgage loan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan") originations, [title insurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Title_insurance "Title insurance") and closing services, [home warranty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Home_warranty "Home warranty"), [property insurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Property_insurance "Property insurance") and [casualty insurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Casualty_insurance "Casualty insurance") and other related services. The company has acquired several residential real estate brokerages including [Long & Foster](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Long_&_Foster "Long & Foster"), acquired in September 2017.[\[72\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-73) In October 1999, Berkshire acquired [Jordan's Furniture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jordan's_Furniture "Jordan's Furniture") for an estimated $200 million to $300 million.[\[73\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-74) CORT Business Services Corporation was acquired in February 2000 by This move was touted as an example of Warren Buffett's "disdain" for investment bankers.[\[122\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-123) In January 2026, Berkshire acquired OxyChem from [Occidental Petroleum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occidental_Petroleum "Occidental Petroleum") for $9.7 billion in cash.[\[123\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-124)[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-125) ### Major stock purchases and sales Buffett began investing in [Wells Fargo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo") in 1989; however, by the first quarter of 2022, Berkshire Hathaway had sold its entire interest in the company.[\[125\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-126)[\[126\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-127) Buffett turned down an opportunity to invest in [Amazon.com](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com") in 1994, as he was unable to predict the company's future growth and it was losing money.[\[127\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-128) Berkshire Hathaway bought shares in Amazon in 2019, a position worth $2 billion at the time.[\[128\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-129) Berkshire made its first investment in [Tesco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tesco "Tesco") in 2006, and in 2012 it raised this stake to over 5% of the company, investing a total of $2.3 billion.[\[129\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-130) Buffett sold around 30% of this stake in 2013 when he "soured somewhat on the company's then-management", realizing a profit of $43 million.[\[130\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-sucking-131) As Tesco's problems mounted through 2014, Berkshire sold all the remaining shares with Buffett saying to shareholders that the delay in selling shares was costly. Berkshire realized an after-tax loss of $444 million on the Tesco investment.[\[130\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-sucking-131) In early 2008, Berkshire increased its stake in [ConocoPhillips](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ConocoPhillips "ConocoPhillips") to 85 million shares.[\[131\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-132) Buffett later described this as "a major mistake" as the [price of oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Price_of_oil "Price of oil") collapsed during the [Great Recession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"). Berkshire sold most of its shares but held 472,000 shares until 2012. That year, ConocoPhillips completed the [corporate spin-off](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporate_spin-off "Corporate spin-off") of [Phillips 66](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phillips_66 "Phillips 66"), of which Berkshire owned 27 million shares. Berkshire later sold $1.4 billion worth of shares to Phillips 66 in exchange for Phillips Specialty Products.[\[132\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-133) Buffett frequently referred to Phillips 66 as one of the best businesses Berkshire invested in because of its consistent dividends and [share repurchase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Share_repurchase "Share repurchase") programs. However, Berkshire sold its entire holdings in 2020.[\[133\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-134)[\[134\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-135) In September 2008, at the peak of the [2008 financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), Berkshire invested $5 billion in [preferred stock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") in [Goldman Sachs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") to provide it with a source of funding when capital markets had become constrained. The preferred stock yielded an annual interest rate of 10%, earning Berkshire $500 million in interest income per year. Berkshire also received warrants to buy 43.5 million shares with at $115 per share, which were exercisable at any time for a five-year term.[\[135\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-136)[\[136\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-137) Goldman had the right to re-purchase the preferred stock at a 10% premium, and in March 2011 exercised this right paying $5.5 billion to Berkshire. Profit on the preferred stock was estimated at $1.8 billion[\[137\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-138) and exercising the warrants yielded a profit of more than $2 billion.[\[138\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-139) Buffett defended [Lloyd Blankfein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lloyd_Blankfein "Lloyd Blankfein")'s decisions as CEO of Goldman Sachs and his efforts to improve the reputation of the company during the [2008 financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[\[139\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-140)[\[140\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-141)[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-142)[\[142\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-GlobalDatabase-143) In September 2008, BHE invested about US$230million for approximately a 10% share of [BYD Auto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BYD_Auto "BYD Auto").[\[143\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-144) Some shares were sold in 2023 and 2024, with Buffett motivated by the [China–United States trade war](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war").[\[144\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-145)[\[145\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-146)[\[146\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-147) In October 2008, Berkshire invested $6.5 billion in [Wrigley Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wrigley_Company "Wrigley Company") as part of a financing package to enable [Mars Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mars_Inc. "Mars Inc.") to acquire the company. Berkshire sold its remaining bonds and preferred stock to Mars in 2016.[\[147\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-148) In early 2011, Berkshire first invested in [IBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM "IBM"). The shares were entirely sold in 2018.[\[148\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-149)[\[149\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-150) On August 26, 2011, Berkshire Hathaway bought $5 billion of preferred shares in [Bank of America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America").[\[150\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-151) The shares yielded 6%, earning Berkshire $300 million in annual interest. Alongside the preferred stock investment, Berkshire obtained warrants allowing Berkshire to buy 700 million common shares at $7.14 per share, which were exercised. By 2017, this position had yielded a profit of about $12 billion excluding the annual interest earned from the preferred stock.[\[151\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-152) In 2013, Berkshire and [3G Capital](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/3G_Capital "3G Capital") acquired [H. J. Heinz Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H._J._Heinz_Company "H. J. Heinz Company") for $28 billion including debt.[\[152\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-153) In 2015, Heinz merged with [Kraft Foods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kraft_Foods "Kraft Foods") to form [Kraft Heinz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kraft_Heinz "Kraft Heinz").[\[153\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-154) In 2019, Buffett said that he overpaid in the deal.[\[154\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-155) In 2013, Berkshire owned 1.74 million shares of [Gannett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gannett "Gannett"); however, it sold its shares in the second quarter of 2013.[\[155\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-156)[\[156\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-157) In the first quarter of 2016, Berkshire began investing in [Apple Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), with a purchase of 9.8 million shares (0.2% of Apple) worth $1 billion. By the end of June 2016, this stake had increased to 15.2 million shares (0.3% of Apple). By December 31, 2016, Berkshire had built up a stake of 57.4 million shares (1.1% of Apple) with an estimated average acquisition price of $110 per share (before the 2020 4:1 split). Aggressive stock purchases continued and by March 31, 2017, Berkshire had amassed a stake of 129 million shares (2.5% of Apple). By December 31, 2017, Berkshire owned 166 million shares (3.3% of Apple). Berkshire bought 75 million more shares in early 2018.[\[157\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-158)[\[158\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-159)As of December31,2022,[\[update\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Berkshire_Hathaway&action=edit) Berkshire owned 5.8% of Apple.[\[159\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-160)[\[160\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-dumps-161) Berkshire Hathaway reduced its Apple stake by nearly 50%, selling $75.5 billion worth of stock in the second quarter of 2024, increasing its cash reserves to a record $276.9 billion.[\[161\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-162) Buffett had said that Apple has developed an ecosystem and level of brand loyalty that provides it with an [economic moat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic_moat "Economic moat"), and that consumers appear to have a degree of price insensitivity when it comes to the [iPhone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IPhone "IPhone"). While Buffett generally does not invest in tech stocks, he has said that Apple is a consumer products company and that he understands consumer products businesses.[\[162\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-163) In the third quarter of 2016, Berkshire surprised investors by making large equity investments in the major US [airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Airline "Airline"). Buffett had previously described airlines as a "deathtrap for investors". Buffett had made an investment in US Airways in 1989 which, although he sold for a profit, almost lost Berkshire a substantial sum of money.[\[163\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-164) In 2017, Berkshire was the largest shareholder in [United Airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines") and [Delta Air Lines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines") and a top 3 shareholder in [Southwest Airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Southwest_Airlines "Southwest Airlines") and [American Airlines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines"). Buffett himself has described this as a "call on the industry" rather than a choice in an individual company. American Airlines' CEO [Doug Parker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Doug_Parker "Doug Parker") is said to have won over Ted Weschler in arguing that the airline industry had consolidated sufficiently and rationalized supply such that longer-term profitability could be achieved in an industry that has historically been loss-making in aggregate.[\[164\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-165) In April 2020, Berkshire sold all shares in the airlines due to the [impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on commercial air transport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_commercial_air_transport "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on commercial air transport").[\[165\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-166) In June 2017, Berkshire invested $377 million in Store Capital, a Scottsdale-based [real estate investment trust](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_estate_investment_trust "Real estate investment trust") that owned more than 1,750 properties in 48 states; the company was sold to [private equity firms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_equity_firm "Private equity firm") in 2022.[\[166\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-167)[\[167\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-168)[\[168\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-169) In June 2017, Berkshire acquired a 38.4% stake in [Home Capital Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Home_Capital_Group "Home Capital Group") for $400 million, giving a lifeline to the [Toronto](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")-based embattled mortgage lender.[\[169\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-170)[\[170\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-171) The shares were sold in 2018.[\[171\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-172) In 2019, by buying warrants and preferred stock, Berkshire provided $10 billion in financing to [Occidental Petroleum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occidental_Petroleum "Occidental Petroleum") as part of its acquisition of [Anadarko Petroleum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anadarko_Petroleum "Anadarko Petroleum"). Berkshire began investing in common shares of Occidental beginning in 2022 and has increased its position since.[\[172\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-173)[\[173\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-174) Between September 2019 and August 2020, Berkshire bought more than 5% of the outstanding stock of each of the five largest Japanese [sogo shosha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sogo_shosha "Sogo shosha") ( [Itochu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Itochu "Itochu"), [Mitsubishi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitsubishi_Corporation "Mitsubishi Corporation"), [Mitsui](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitsui_&_Co. "Mitsui & Co."), [Sumitomo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sumitomo_Corporation "Sumitomo Corporation"), and [Marubeni](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marubeni "Marubeni")) through its National Indemnity subsidiary. These stakes were worth a total of over $6 billion as of August 2020. By April 2023, Berkshire increased its stake in each of those companies to 7.4%.[\[174\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-175) He continued to increase the stakes over time.[\[175\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-176) In the second quarter of 2020, Berkshire added a position of more than 20 million shares in [Barrick Gold](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barrick_Gold "Barrick Gold"); the shares were sold in 2021.[\[176\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-177) In the third quarter of 2020, the company agreed to buy Dominion Energy's natural gas transmission and storage operations.[\[177\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-178) In the first quarter of 2022, Berkshire acquired a $2.6 billion stake in [Paramount Global](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global").[\[178\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-179) However, at an annual meeting on May 4, 2024, Buffett stated that he had sold all of his shares in Paramount at a substantial loss, blaming himself for deciding to invest.[\[179\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-180) In the first quarter of 2022, Berkshire acquired 121 million shares of [HP Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP_Inc. "HP Inc.") valued at more than $4.2 billion; the shares were sold in 2023 and 2024.[\[180\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-181) In the third quarter of 2022, Berkshire bought 60 million shares in [TSMC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TSMC "TSMC") valued at $4.1billion; the shares were sold in late 2022 and 2023 in part due to geopolitical tensions.[\[181\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-182)[\[160\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-dumps-161)[\[182\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-183) Berkshire began investing in [Chubb Limited](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chubb_Limited "Chubb Limited") in 2023; by 2024, it owned a 6.4% stake worth $6.7 billion.[\[183\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-184) Berkshire Hathaway bought 5.62 million shares of [Constellation Brands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constellation_Brands "Constellation Brands") in the fourth quarter of 2024, worth $1.24 billion. The purchase made Berkshire Hathaway the sixth-largest shareholder of Constellation Brands. Berkshire Hathaway sold its stake in [Ulta Beauty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ulta_Beauty "Ulta Beauty") in the fourth quarter of 2024, just two quarters after acquiring the shares.[\[184\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-185) In the fourth quarter of 2024, Berkshire Hathaway liquidated all of its ETF holdings in the S&P 500, worth a total of $44 million.[\[185\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-186) ## "NVR, Inc.") | 0.4 | $0.1 billion | | [Allegion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Allegion "Allegion") | 0.9 | $0.1 billion | The company also had $381.7 billion in cash and Treasury bills.[\[188\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-tracker-189) ## Corporate affairs ### Investment strategy [Insurance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insurance "Insurance") is a major area of operations and the retained premiums (float) serve as an important source of capital.[\[189\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-191) Buffett has said that he prefers to invest in evergreen businesses that generate predictable long-term returns,[\[190\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-192) that he doesn't invest in companies that he doesn't understand,[\[191\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-193) and that he does not like to invest in companies that may undergo significant change.[\[192\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-194) The company has generally avoided investing in [high-tech](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-tech "High-tech") firms, missing early opportunities to invest in [Microsoft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") and [Amazon.com](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com") despite having relationships with the founders of those companies. With some exceptions, the company also usually does not invest in [real property](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_property "Real property") due to precise pricing, lack of competitive advantage, complex management, and corporation tax disadvantages.[\[193\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-195) ### Share classes and aversion to stock splits Berkshire Hathaway has never undergone a [stock split](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stock_split "Stock split") of its Class A shares because of management's desire to attract long-term investors as opposed to short-term [speculation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speculation "Speculation").[\[194\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-196) However, in 1996, Berkshire Hathaway created [Class B shares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_B_share "Class B share"), with a per-share value of 1⁄30 of that of the original shares (now Class A) and 1⁄200 of the per-share voting rights, and after the January 2010 split, at 1⁄1,500 the price and 1⁄10,000 the voting rights of the Class-A shares. Holders of class A stock are allowed to convert their stock to Class B, though not vice versa. Buffett was reluctant to create the class B shares but did so to thwart the creation of [unit investment trusts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_investment_trust "Unit investment trust") that would have marketed themselves as Berkshire look-alikes.[\[195\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-197) In January 2010, as part of the acquisition of [BNSF Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BNSF_Railway "BNSF Railway"), Berkshire completed a 50-to-1 stock split of its [class B shares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Class_B_share "Class B share"). The increased [market liquidity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Market_liquidity "Market liquidity") resulted in the company's inclusion in the [S&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S&P_500 "S&P 500").[\[196\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-198)[\[197\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-199) ### Share price milestones Shares closed over $1,000 for the first time on August 26, 1983, and over $10,000 on October 16, 1992.[\[198\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-milestone-200) Shares closed over $100,000 for the first time on October 23, 2006.[\[199\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-201) They first closed over $200,000 on August 14, 2014.[\[198\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-milestone-200) Shares closed over $500,000 for the first time on March 16, 2022.[\[200\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-202) ### Executive compensation The salary for Buffett is $100,000 per year with no stock options,[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14A-5) which is among the lowest salaries[\[201\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-203) for CEOs of large companies in the United States.[\[202\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-204) Buffett's salary has not changed in 35 years.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14A-5) Buffett also receives approximately $300,000 worth of home security services from the company annually.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14A-5) Abel and Jain each receive a salary of $20 million per year, with a possible $3 million annual bonus at the discretion of Buffett.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-14A-5) ### Annual letters and shareholders' meetings [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/2018_Berkshire_Sunday_at_Borsheims_-_41039852065.jpg/500px-2018_Berkshire_Sunday_at_Borsheims_-_41039852065.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:2018_Berkshire_Sunday_at_Borsheims_-_41039852065.jpg) Berkshire routinely features Coca-Cola products, from one of its oldest and largest investments, at its corporate events. Buffett's letters to shareholders are published annually. Berkshire's annual shareholders' meetings, nicknamed " [Woodstock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Woodstock "Woodstock") for Capitalists", take place at the [CHI Health Center Omaha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CHI_Health_Center_Omaha "CHI Health Center Omaha") in Omaha, Nebraska. Attendance generally totals over 40,000 people and the meetings last for 6 to 8 hours, with Buffett himself, as well as Abel and Jain, answering questions from shareholders.[\[203\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-205)[\[204\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-206) Known for their humor and light-heartedness, the meetings typically start with a cartoon made for Berkshire shareholders. - The 2004 cartoon featured [Arnold Schwarzenegger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") in the role of "The Warrenator" who travels through time to stop Buffett and Munger's attempt to save the world from a "mega" corporation formed by [Microsoft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")- [Starbucks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Starbucks "Starbucks")- [Wal-Mart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wal-Mart "Wal-Mart"). Schwarzenegger is later shown arguing in a gym with Buffett regarding [Proposition 13](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_Proposition_13_(1978) "California Proposition 13 (1978)").[\[205\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-207) - The 2006 cartoon depicted actresses [Jamie Lee Curtis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jamie_Lee_Curtis "Jamie Lee Curtis") and [Nicollette Sheridan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicollette_Sheridan "Nicollette Sheridan") lusting after Munger.[\[206\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-208)[\[207\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-209) - The 2007 cartoon included Buffett playing basketball against [LeBron James](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James").[\[208\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-210)[\[209\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-211) - The 2008 cartoon depicted [Charlie Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Munger "Charlie Munger") becoming [President of the United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), after which he promotes eating at [Dairy Queen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dairy_Queen "Dairy Queen") to relieve the effects of increased temperatures due to [climate change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[\[210\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-212) - The 2009 cartoon, aired just after the [2008 financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"), showed a new mattress, named The Nervous Nellie, which has a hidden compartment for people who prefer to hide their money in the mattress than put it in the bank.[\[211\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-213) - The 2016 cartoon featured a spoof of the 1983 film _[Trading Places](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trading_Places "Trading Places")_; in this version, Munger and Jain along with the [GEICO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GEICO "GEICO") gecko conspire to manipulate the market for cocoa beans to affect See's Candy.[\[212\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-214) - The 2022 cartoon featured a veterinarian who expanded his business using his American Express credit card.[\[213\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-215) ### Governance Current members of the board of directors of Berkshire Hathaway are [Warren Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Warren_Buffett "Warren Buffett") (chairman), [Greg Abel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Abel "Greg Abel") (vice chairman of non-insurance business operations), [Ajit Jain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Jain "Ajit Jain") (vice chairman of insurance operations), Chris Davis, [Susan Alice Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susan_Alice_Buffett "Susan Alice Buffett") (Buffett's daughter), [Howard Graham Buffett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_Graham_Buffett "Howard Graham Buffett") (Buffett's son), [Ronald Olson](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ronald_Olson "Ronald Olson"), [Kenneth Chenault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kenneth_Chenault "Kenneth Chenault"), [Steve Burke](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steve_Burke_(businessman) "Steve Burke (businessman)"), [Susan Decker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Susan_Decker "Susan Decker"), Meryl Witmer, Charlotte Guyman, Esteban Rodriguez Jr.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-10K-4) [Charlie Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_Munger "Charlie Munger") served as vice chairman of the company from 1978 until his death on November 28, 2023.[\[214\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-216) [David L. Sokol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_L._Sokol "David L. Sokol"), CEO of [Berkshire Hathaway Energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway_Energy "Berkshire Hathaway Energy") until early 2008,[\[215\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-217) was a top lieutenant for Buffett. Sokol, who was paid $24 million per year, resigned from Berkshire in 2011 after it was disclosed that he personally made a $3 million profit from Berkshire Hathaway's purchase of [Lubrizol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lubrizol "Lubrizol").[\[216\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-218) In October 2010, Berkshire announced that 39-year old [Todd Combs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Todd_Combs "Todd Combs"), manager of the [hedge fund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") Castle Point Capital, would join as an [investment manager](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Investment_manager "Investment manager").[\[217\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-219)[\[218\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-220)[\[219\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-221)[\[220\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-222) In early 2012, 50-year-old [Ted Weschler](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ted_Weschler "Ted Weschler"), founder of Peninsula Capital Advisors, joined Berkshire as a second investment manager.[\[221\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-223)[\[222\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-224) In January 2018, Berkshire Hathaway appointed [Ajit Jain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ajit_Jain "Ajit Jain") and [Greg Abel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Abel "Greg Abel") to vice-chairman roles. Abel was appointed vice chairman for non-insurance business operations, and Jain became vice chairman of insurance operations.[\[223\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-225)[\[224\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-226) In May 2021, Buffett named [Greg Abel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Abel "Greg Abel") to be his successor as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway.[\[225\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-227) In May 2025, Buffett announced his intention to retire as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway at the annual shareholders' meeting; Greg Abel succeeded him on January 1, 2026.[\[226\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-228)[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-Abel-CEO-6) ### Financials | Year[\[227\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway#cite_note-229) | Revenue

# Charlie Munger Charlie Munger American businessman (1924–2023) "Charles Munger" redirects here. For the politician, see [Charles Henry Munger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Henry_Munger "Charles Henry Munger"). | Charlie Munger | | --- | | [![Munger seated, wearing a suit](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/56/Charlie_Munger_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Charlie_Munger_(cropped).jpg)
Munger in 2010 | | Born | Charles Thomas Munger
(1924-01-01)January 1, 1924
[Omaha, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska"), U.S. | | Died | November 28, 2023(2023-11-28) (aged99)
[Santa Barbara, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Santa_Barbara,_California "Santa Barbara, California"), U.S. | | Education | - [University of Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan")
- [California Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology")

- financial analyst

- philanthropist
- lawyer | | Knownfor | Vice Chairman of [Berkshire Hathaway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") | | Spouses | Nancy Jean Huggins
